title: Tenant/Workspace Isolation Design Pack
context: GrantLayer is publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/Discodone/grantlayer.git in a Developer Preview / controlled-pilot posture. GL-199 identified tenant/workspace isolation as the highest-priority production blocker (P0). GL-200A is the design pack only — it does not implement tenant/workspace isolation. It defines architecture, scope, risks, migration strategy, and the implementation plan for GL-200B and later issues.
current_state_summary: GrantLayer has no production tenant/workspace isolation implemented. All grants, grant requests, grant executions, challenges, evidence archives, provenance events, and audit events share a single namespace within any given deployment. There is no tenant_id, workspace_id, or any tenant/workspace entity in the data model, schema, or API. Auth provides admin-token mode (dev/demo) and operator-token mode (hardened). Neither mode carries tenant/workspace context. Agent permission scopes are globally-scoped within a deployment. The only boundary today is the operator-bounded workspace model from GL-132 Option B. This state is acceptable for Developer Preview and Controlled Preview under strict boundaries (GL-198). It is not acceptable for Production SaaS with unrelated customers on shared infrastructure.

terminology.tenant: The top-level logical isolation boundary in GrantLayer. Isolates one customer's or institution's data — grants, grant requests, grant executions, evidence, audit records, and operator identities — from all other customers. Maps to a university, foundation, corporation, or any institution that controls a bounded set of grants and operators.
terminology.workspace: A scoped operating context within a tenant. Maps to a team, department, program office, or organizational unit inside the tenant that shares grants and evidence but remains isolated from other workspaces within the same tenant. Optional in the GL-200B minimum viable implementation.
terminology.actor: Any entity — human operator, automated agent, or admin — that performs an action tracked by GrantLayer. Actors must be bound to a tenant at minimum; workspaces are optional in the GL-200B baseline.
terminology.operator: An authenticated individual or service identity that performs actions within a tenant/workspace. Carries a role (owner, grant_admin, grant_reviewer, readonly, auditor) that constrains permitted actions within their tenant/workspace boundary.
terminology.admin: An operator with elevated privileges (owner or grant_admin) who can manage tenant configuration, membership, and boundary policies. System-level admin actions crossing tenants must be explicitly marked and restricted.
terminology.agent: An automated software entity (e.g., LangChain agent, Claude-powered AI) that performs grant workflow actions on behalf of an operator. Agents inherit their owner's tenant/workspace context via agent permission assignments.
terminology.grant: A time-bounded authorization record permitting a subject to perform a specific action on a specific resource. In a tenant-aware model, every grant must carry a tenant_id and optionally a workspace_id.
terminology.grant_request: A structured request for a grant submitted by an operator and subject to approval workflow. Must be scoped to the same tenant/workspace as the operator who submitted it.
terminology.challenge: A cryptographic proof-of-intent token created during grant execution. Must be scoped to the same tenant/workspace as the actor and grant they are associated with.
terminology.execution: A grant execution record of a protected action attempt — authorized, denied, or pending. Must carry the same tenant/workspace context as the grant that produced it.
terminology.evidence: The collection of artifacts — files, verification records, attestations — that document the outcome of a grant execution. Must be scoped to the same workspace (and tenant) as the execution.
terminology.audit_event: An immutable, hash-chained record of every authorization decision. Must carry tenant/workspace context. System-level events (migrations, health checks) are explicitly marked as scope: system.

threat_model.vector: Operator A queries grant list; no tenant filter applied
Operator A creates grant for tenant B resources
Attacker with valid operator token accesses other tenant
threat_model.required_mitigation: Bind operator token to tenant_id at creation

threat_model.vector: Workspace A user reads workspace B grants
Auditor reads another tenant's audit trail
threat_model.required_mitigation: tenant_id on audit events + filtered queries

threat_model.vector: Agent with grant:read scope reads all tenants
System admin reads any tenant data
Demo endpoint reveals real tenant data
threat_model.required_mitigation: Demo endpoint serves only synthetic data (GL-190 guard maintained)
Idempotent migration with explicit tenant assignment

isolation_goals: Every business resource (grant, grant_request, grant_execution, challenge, evidence, audit_event, agent_permission_assignment) must carry an explicit tenant_id in the database schema
Every API query or mutation involving business resources must filter by the requesting actor's tenant_id; cross-tenant access must be denied
Operators must be bound to a tenant_id at creation; operator tokens must carry tenant context in the auth layer
Agent permission scopes must be scoped to the operator's tenant context
Audit events must record tenant context; the audit chain must be tenant-aware
Demo, health, and readiness endpoints are public by design and remain outside tenant scope
System/admin operations requiring cross-tenant access must be explicitly marked, logged, and restricted to named admin roles
The design must support both SQLite (dev/test) and PostgreSQL (production) backends
isolation_non_goals: Full RBAC/policy engine (deferred to GL-200D or later)
Per-workspace row-level security at the DB layer (deferred)
Frontend tenant onboarding UI (not in scope)
Customer-facing tenant admin panel (not in scope)
Federated identity / SSO per tenant (not in scope)
Tenant-level rate limiting (not in scope for GL-200B)
Workspace sub-isolation as a hard requirement in the GL-200B baseline

resource_scoping_matrix.recommended_gl200b_action: Add tenant_id column; filter all queries; workspace_id reserved nullable
Add tenant_id column; filter all queries; workspace_id reserved nullable
Add tenant_id column; filter queries; workspace_id reserved nullable
Add tenant_id column; filter queries; workspace_id reserved nullable
Add tenant_id column; filter queries; workspace_id reserved nullable
Add tenant_id column; system events get scope: system; update hash-chain canonical payload
Add tenant_id column; operator bound to tenant at creation
Ensure operator lookup enforces tenant context

resource_scoping_matrix.required_future_scope: tenant (assignments scoped; profiles may remain global templates)
resource_scoping_matrix.recommended_gl200b_action: Add tenant_id to assignments; profiles remain global scope-vocabulary templates
Add tenant_id column; enforce tenant context in assignment queries
Document synthetic-data-only; maintain GL-190 guard; no tenant scoping
No tenant-scoping needed; per-deployment config sufficient
Migrations must add tenant columns safely; idempotent backfill strategy required

design_options.name: tenant_id only baseline
design_options.description: Add a tenant_id column to all business resource tables. All API queries filter by the requesting operator's tenant_id. No workspace sub-boundary. Operator tokens are bound to a tenant_id at creation.
benefits: Minimal schema change (one new column per table)
Fail-closed by design: missing tenant_id on operator → deny
Compatible with both SQLite and PostgreSQL
risks: No workspace sub-isolation; all operators in a tenant share access
Workspace isolation deferred — must not be claimed as implemented
Tenant creation/management API not included; tenants are pre-provisioned

design_options.name: tenant_id + workspace_id on all business resources
design_options.description: Add both tenant_id and workspace_id to all business resource tables. Operators are bound to both a tenant and a workspace at creation. All API queries filter by both.
benefits: Full two-level isolation from the start
Workspace-level export, audit, and evidence boundaries
No technical debt when workspace isolation is eventually required
risks: Significantly higher implementation complexity than Option A
Every query, model, and API endpoint must carry workspace context
Migration complexity doubles (two columns, two backfill dimensions)
Risk of shipping a partially-correct implementation under time pressure
design_options.suitability_for_gl200b: medium — recommended only if workspace isolation is required before controlled preview moves to production

design_options.name: request-context isolation layer with staged DB migration
design_options.description: Add a middleware/request-context layer that extracts and validates tenant context from the auth token on every request. DB schema changes (adding tenant_id) are staged in a separate migration. Enforcement is applied at the query layer via a context object.
benefits: Decouples schema migration from enforcement rollout
Context object is a clean seam for future workspace support
Enables incremental rollout: enforce at API layer first, then migrate schema
risks: Two-phase rollout creates a window where schema is migrated but enforcement is incomplete
Context object must be threaded through every resource function — high refactor surface area
Higher risk of incomplete enforcement if staging is not carefully managed
design_options.suitability_for_gl200b: medium — valuable pattern but adds complexity

design_options.name: full RBAC/tenant/workspace policy engine
design_options.description: Implement a complete policy engine with tenant, workspace, role, and resource-level access control. Operators, agents, and admins are assigned policies. The policy engine evaluates every access decision against the policy store.
benefits: Future-proof for complex institutional permission hierarchies
risks: Very high implementation complexity — likely 4-8x more work than Option A
Policy store becomes a critical dependency; misconfiguration = data breach
Cannot be completed in GL-200B scope; would require multiple issues
Premature for current scale; over-engineering risk is high
design_options.suitability_for_gl200b: low — deferred to GL-200D or later

recommended_design.summary: GL-200B implements Option A: tenant_id on all business resource tables, operator tokens bound to tenant_id, API queries filtered by tenant_id, audit events include tenant_id, fail-closed enforcement on missing/ambiguous tenant context. workspace_id column is reserved nullable in the GL-200B migration to avoid a second disruptive schema change. workspace_id enforcement is staged to GL-200C.
recommended_design.rationale: Unblocks production readiness fastest. Minimal schema change with clear migration path. Fail-closed by design. Leaves clean seam for workspace support in GL-200C. Does not over-engineer before scale requires it.

api_boundary_model.tenant_context_entry: Tenant context must enter every API request through the authenticated operator identity. Tenant context is resolved server-side from the operator record — clients do not send a tenant header.
api_boundary_model.admin_context: System-level admin operations that require cross-tenant access must use an explicit is_system_admin flag on the operator record and must be separately logged.

fail_closed_behavior.resource_in_different_tenant: deny with 404 (not 403) to avoid leaking tenant existence

api_boundary_model.error_response_safety: Error responses must never include raw tenant_id values from other tenants, resource IDs from other tenants, operator tokens, or secrets. Generic error messages are used for cross-tenant denial.

current_state.admin_token_mode: GRANTLAYER_ADMIN_TOKEN env var; no tenant context
current_state.operator_token_mode: operator record has id, name, role, token_hash, expires_at; no tenant_id

required_for_gl200b: Operator table gains tenant_id — a non-null column binding each operator to exactly one tenant
Operator auth returns tenant context — authenticate_operator() returns the operator's tenant_id alongside their role
Admin token mode in GL-200B remains a dev/demo fallback bound to a single dev tenant context; must not be used in production multi-tenant deployments
Agent permission assignments gain tenant_id — assignments are scoped to the operator's tenant
minimum_gl200b_enforcement: authenticate_operator() resolves tenant_id from operator record
Auth guard injects tenant_id into request context
All business resource queries include WHERE tenant_id = ? clause
deferred: OAuth 2.0 / JWT / SSO per tenant — deferred to production auth issue
Workspace-level operator membership — deferred to GL-200C
Tenant provisioning API — deferred (tenants are pre-provisioned in GL-200B)

all_business_resource_tables: workspace_id TEXT DEFAULT NULL (reserved for GL-200C)

database_data_model_strategy.backfill_strategy: Existing demo/dev data must be backfilled to tenant_id = 'demo'. Migration steps: (1) Add tenant_id TEXT DEFAULT 'unassigned' to each table; (2) UPDATE <table> SET tenant_id = 'demo' WHERE tenant_id = 'unassigned'; (3) Add workspace_id TEXT DEFAULT NULL (reserved); (4) Add index on tenant_id per table.
database_data_model_strategy.migration_safety: Migrations must be idempotent (re-run does not fail). No data is deleted during migration. SQLite: check PRAGMA table_info before ALTER TABLE. PostgreSQL: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS. No new UNIQUE constraints on tenant_id alone.
database_data_model_strategy.sqlite_postgresql_implications: SQLite: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN supported without IF NOT EXISTS; must check PRAGMA table_info first. PostgreSQL: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS supported (v9.6+). Both backends produce identical query behaviour via existing _translate_placeholders layer in db.py.
indexes: CREATE INDEX idx_grants_tenant_subject ON grants(tenant_id, subject_id)
Similar indexes for each scoped table
database_data_model_strategy.uniqueness_constraints: No new UNIQUE constraints needed in GL-200B (IDs remain globally unique UUIDs). Composite uniqueness constraints (if any) would be (tenant_id, id) but UUIDs are globally unique so this is not required.
database_data_model_strategy.cascading_behavior: No cascade deletes in GL-200B. Tenant deactivation is a future concern.
database_data_model_strategy.audit_immutability: The hash-chain in audit_events covers a canonical JSON payload. Adding tenant_id to the payload breaks the existing chain unless handled carefully. Recommended: new events after migration include tenant_id in canonical hash payload; historical events retain original row_hash; migration boundary marker event appended to chain.
database_data_model_strategy.rollback_compatibility: Rollback of tenant_id migration is low-risk (column drop) as long as no non-demo data has been written under tenant context. Pre-migration clients continue to work via admin-token/operator-token path (which resolves tenant context server-side).

audit_design.tenant_context_in_events: Every audit event written after GL-200B implementation must include: tenant_id (the tenant context of the action), workspace_id (nullable in GL-200B), scope (one of: tenant, tenant_admin, system, system_admin, public).
audit_design.system_events: System-level events (migration completion, health probes) use scope: system, tenant_id: null.
audit_design.immutable_audit_behavior: Audit events remain append-only and tamper-evident after GL-200B. Hash-chain mechanism is preserved. Migration boundary marker event handles the transition. tenant_id is included in canonical hash payload for post-migration events.
audit_design.no_sensitive_data_in_audit: Raw token values must never appear in audit events. Raw grant payloads must not be embedded. No PII beyond what is already logged.
audit_design.tenant_workspace_context_propagation: All business resource mutations write audit events with tenant_id. System operations write audit events with scope: system and tenant_id: null. Cross-tenant denial events are always written regardless of deny outcome.

permission_model_implications.agent_permission_scopes: Current scopes (evidence:read, grant_request:read, etc.) are globally-scoped within a deployment. After GL-200B: agent permission assignments carry the operator's tenant_id; scope evaluation must receive tenant context and verify that the requested resource belongs to the same tenant as the assignment. Scope vocabulary does not change. Wildcard scopes (*:*) remain valid but are tenant-bounded.
permission_model_implications.operator_role_model: Operator roles (owner, grant_admin, grant_reviewer, readonly, auditor) remain unchanged. Roles are enforced within the tenant context. Cross-tenant role elevation is forbidden.
permission_model_implications.admin_token_mode: In GL-200B, admin token mode may remain as a dev/demo fallback bound to a single dev tenant. Must not be used in production multi-tenant deployments. Must be clearly documented as not production-safe.

cross_tenant_isolation_tests.description: Operator A (tenant_1) cannot read grants belonging to tenant_2
Operator A (tenant_1) cannot write grants to tenant_2
Operator A (tenant_1) cannot read audit events from tenant_2
Operator A (tenant_1) cannot read evidence from tenant_2
Operator A (tenant_1) cannot read grant requests from tenant_2
Cross-tenant access denial is auditable (audit event written)
Cross-tenant denial returns 404 (not 403) for resource endpoints

workspace_isolation_tests.description: Workspace A operator cannot read grants from workspace B within same tenant (GL-200C)
Workspace A operator cannot write grants to workspace B (GL-200C)

auth_context_tests.description: Admin token mode resolves to dev tenant context

audit_context_tests.description: Cross-tenant denial events are written to audit log
Audit chain verification handles pre/post-migration events

demo_endpoint_safety_tests.description: /demo/* endpoints remain accessible without auth (GL-190 guard)
/demo/* endpoints do not expose tenant-scoped data

health_readiness_tests.description: Health/readiness responses contain no tenant-scoped data

migration_tests.description: Migration adds tenant_id to all business resource tables
Migration backfill sets existing rows to tenant_id = demo
Migration is idempotent (re-run does not fail)
Existing test fixtures continue to pass post-migration

examples_determinism_tests.description: Public examples remain deterministic after implementation

security_boundary_regression_tests.description: Anonymous access remains denied where expected
No raw token/secret leakage in any API response
Security boundary regression suite passes with no new failures

rollout_plan.phase_1_gl200b: Add tenant_id (non-null, backfilled) and workspace_id (nullable, reserved) columns to all business resource tables via new migration. Update operators table. Update authenticate_operator() to return tenant_id. Inject tenant context into all business resource queries. Add fail-closed guard: missing tenant_id → 403. Update audit event writer to include tenant_id. Update hash-chain canonical payload for post-migration events. Write and pass all T-xxx, A-xxx, AU-xxx, M-xxx tests. Update docs after merge.
rollout_plan.phase_2_gl200c: Activate workspace_id enforcement on all business resources. Add workspace membership model. Write and pass all W-xxx tests.
rollout_plan.phase_3_gl200d: Implement full RBAC/tenant/workspace policy engine. Replace per-query tenant filter with policy evaluation layer.
rollout_plan.phase_4_gl201: Update docs/openapi.yaml to reflect tenant context in API contract.
rollout_plan.phase_5_gl202: Harden audit event tenant context; add workspace_id to audit. Verify chain integrity across migrations.
rollout_plan.phase_6_gl203: Add cross-tenant and workspace isolation to public smoke matrix. Verify all T-xxx, W-xxx, AU-xxx tests in CI.

compatibility_strategy.no_breaking_api_change_in_gl200b: Tenant context is resolved server-side from the operator record; clients do not need to change.
compatibility_strategy.existing_examples_remain_deterministic: Examples use a single operator/tenant (demo); no change needed.
compatibility_strategy.existing_test_fixtures_continue: Test DB is initialized fresh per test; migration runs as part of init_db(); all existing tests remain valid.
compatibility_strategy.sqlite_postgresql_parity: Migration must work on both backends.
compatibility_strategy.admin_token_dev_demo_mode: Remains available as a single-tenant fallback for development; must not be used in multi-tenant production.

production_readiness_criteria: DB schema scoped — tenant_id column exists on all business resource tables in production schema
Request context enforced — every API request that accesses business resources reads and enforces the operator's tenant_id
All sensitive resources scoped — grants, grant_requests, challenges, grant_executions, evidence/executions, audit_events, operators, agent_permission_assignments all have tenant_id enforced
Cross-tenant tests pass — all T-xxx tests pass in CI
Audit context propagated — all AU-xxx tests pass; audit events include tenant_id
Migration verified — all M-xxx tests pass; migration is idempotent
Examples deterministic — all E-xxx tests pass
Security boundary regression passes — all S-xxx tests pass with no new failures
Public docs updated — docs updated only after GL-200B is merged and verified, not before
Security review complete — security-sensitive findings routed to GitHub Security Advisories before public disclosure

gl200b.title: Tenant/Workspace Isolation Implementation Baseline
gl200b.scope: Implement tenant_id on all business resource tables, operator binding, auth context injection, query filtering, and audit context. Write all T-xxx, A-xxx, AU-xxx, M-xxx tests.
deliverables: New DB migration adding tenant_id, workspace_id (nullable) to all tables
Updated resource query functions with tenant_id filter
Updated audit event writer with tenant_id

gl200c.title: Workspace Isolation Baseline
gl200c.scope: Activate workspace_id enforcement; add workspace membership model; write W-xxx tests.

gl200d.title: Tenant/Workspace Policy Engine
gl200d.scope: Implement full RBAC/policy engine replacing per-query tenant filter.

gl201.title: Tenant/Workspace Isolation API Contract Update
gl201.scope: Update docs/openapi.yaml to reflect tenant context.

gl202.title: Tenant/Workspace Isolation Audit Propagation
gl202.scope: Harden audit chain tenant context; dual-mode chain verification; workspace_id in audit canonical payload.

gl203.title: Tenant/Workspace Isolation Regression/Smoke Matrix
gl203.scope: Add cross-tenant and workspace isolation to CI smoke matrix.

findings.summary: No tenant_id column exists on any business resource table
findings.evidence: backend/src/migrations/0001_gl032_baseline.py: grants, grant_requests, grant_executions, challenges, audit_events, operators tables have no tenant_id column. Zero occurrences of tenant_id in backend/src/.
findings.recommended_action: Add tenant_id column to all business resource tables in a new GL-200B migration. Backfill existing data to demo tenant.

findings.summary: Operator token carries no tenant_id; auth layer returns no tenant context
findings.evidence: backend/src/auth.py: check_auth() returns operator dict with no tenant_id field. Operator model has no tenant_id column. Auth context cannot enforce tenant scope.
findings.recommended_action: Add tenant_id to operator table and record; update authenticate_operator() to return tenant_id; inject into request context.

findings.summary: All API queries are globally-scoped; no WHERE tenant_id = ? clause exists in any resource query
findings.evidence: backend/src/grants.py: list_grants() = SELECT * FROM grants ORDER BY created_at DESC — no tenant filter. Similar pattern in grant_requests.py, challenges.py, grant_executions.py.
findings.recommended_action: Update all list/get/create/update/delete query functions to accept and apply tenant_id parameter.

findings.summary: Audit events carry no tenant_id; audit trail is a single global namespace
findings.evidence: backend/src/audit_log.py: AuditEvent dataclass and hash-chain payload include no tenant_id. audit_events table schema (migration 0001) has no tenant_id column.
findings.recommended_action: Add tenant_id to audit_events table; update AuditEvent model; update hash-chain canonical payload to include tenant_id for post-migration events; handle dual-mode verification.

findings.summary: Agent permission assignments are globally-scoped; no tenant binding exists
findings.evidence: backend/src/agent_permission_assignments.py: assignments table has no tenant_id column. Scope evaluation does not check tenant context.
findings.recommended_action: Add tenant_id to agent_permission_assignments table; update scope evaluation to verify tenant context.

findings.summary: Hash-chain canonical payload does not include tenant_id; adding it post-migration will break existing chain verification unless handled carefully
findings.evidence: backend/src/audit_log.py: _hash_payload() computes a canonical JSON payload with no tenant_id. Adding tenant_id post-hoc breaks row_hash values for historical events.
findings.recommended_action: Use dual-mode chain verification: historical events (no tenant_id in hash) verified with old canonical payload; new events verified with tenant_id-inclusive payload. Migration boundary marker event appended.

findings.summary: No cross-tenant isolation tests exist in the current test suite
findings.evidence: backend/tests/: no test file contains cross_tenant or tenant_id assertions. Security boundary tests check auth but not tenant data isolation.
findings.recommended_action: Write T-001 through T-007 tests as part of GL-200B. Add to CI smoke matrix in GL-203.

findings.summary: Public documentation correctly states tenant isolation is not implemented; must remain accurate until GL-200B is merged and verified
findings.evidence: docs/tenant_workspace_boundary_decision.md (GL-132): 'No multi-tenant isolation implemented.' SECURITY.md section 6 notes same. docs/production_readiness_gap_report_v2.md confirms not ready.
findings.recommended_action: Do not update public docs to claim tenant isolation until GL-200B is merged, tested, and verified. Then update in GL-200B PR.

findings.summary: workspace_id not in current schema; design must reserve it to avoid a second disruptive migration
findings.evidence: No workspace_id in any migration file. GL-144 design doc proposed workspace model but was never implemented.
findings.recommended_action: Add workspace_id TEXT DEFAULT NULL to all tables in the GL-200B migration to reserve the column. Do not enforce it in GL-200B.

decision_rationale: The design pack is complete. The recommended implementation plan is: GL-200B implements Option A baseline (tenant_id on all business resource tables, operator binding, auth context injection, API query filtering, audit context, fail-closed enforcement). workspace_id column is reserved (nullable) in the GL-200B migration to avoid a second disruptive schema change. Workspace enforcement is staged to GL-200C. The design is compatible with both SQLite and PostgreSQL backends. The hash-chain audit integrity concern (GL-200A-F006) is addressed via dual-mode verification and a migration boundary marker event. All nine findings are actionable and assigned to concrete follow-up issues. No production SaaS claim is made.
